questions.question: Why does a 2D+pool video model fail on the Something-Something V2 dataset even when it achieves strong results on Kinetics-400?
options: The images in Something-Something V2 are too small for ResNet feature extraction
The model architecture is incompatible with the Something-Something label format

Something-Something labels are defined by motion direction ('pushing something from left to right'), not appearance; order-invariant average pooling over per-frame features cannot distinguish motion direction because pool(f1, f2, …, fT) == pool(fT, …, f1)
There are too many classes in Something-Something V2 for a single-head classifier
questions.explanation: Something-Something labels are literally 'X moved left to right' vs 'X moved right to left'. The two actions look identical in any single frame, and averaging frame embeddings is order-invariant. Motion-defined labels require a model that respects temporal order — a 3D conv or spatio-temporal transformer.

questions.question: In `inflate_2d_to_3d`, the inflated 3D weights are divided by `time_kernel` after repeating the 2D weights along the new time axis. Why is this division necessary?
options: To reduce the rank of the weight tensor so it fits in GPU memory

To keep activation magnitudes constant so that batch-normalisation statistics from the pretrained 2D model remain valid on the first 3D forward pass — without the rescale, each output unit would sum `time_kernel` copies of the 2D response, inflating activations by that factor
To prevent exploding gradients during the first backward pass through the inflated layers
To make the 3D kernel mathematically equivalent to a depthwise-separable convolution
questions.explanation: Inflation copies each 2D filter `time_kernel` times along the time axis, so without rescaling every output neuron accumulates `time_kernel` times as much signal. Dividing by `time_kernel` preserves the expected activation magnitude, which is critical when the downstream BatchNorm layers were calibrated on the 2D pretrained statistics.

questions.question: Dense frame sampling picks a random contiguous T-frame window rather than uniformly spacing T frames across the full clip. When should dense sampling be preferred over uniform sampling?
options: For appearance-based tasks where any single frame carries enough signal; uniform sampling covers more of the clip's content
When the video is very long and memory is limited, because a contiguous window is cheaper to decode

For 3D convolutional models that model short-range motion — consecutive frames share temporal context that uniform sampling across the full clip would destroy, leaving large temporal gaps between adjacent model inputs
When the video transformer uses divided attention instead of joint spatio-temporal attention
questions.explanation: 3D convolutions and (2+1)D convolutions have small temporal receptive fields (3–7 frames). They need adjacent frames to capture motion. Uniform sampling over a 5-minute video would space selected frames minutes apart, making temporal kernels see unrelated content. Dense sampling keeps frames neighbouring in time — matching the model's temporal receptive field to the data.

questions.question: In the `FramePool` forward method, the input `x` of shape `(N, T, 3, H, W)` is reshaped to `(N*T, 3, H, W)` before passing through the ResNet backbone. What does this accomplish and why?
options: All T frames from all N videos are processed in one batch-parallel call through the 2D CNN; the output is then unflattened back to `(N, T, 512)` and averaged over the time dimension to produce a single per-video embedding
It converts the tensor from NHWC to NCHW channel ordering before the first conv layer
It applies temporal convolutions across consecutive frames by treating time as the height dimension
It reduces memory usage by processing frames sequentially on CPU rather than in parallel on GPU
questions.explanation: By collapsing the batch and time axes, all N*T frames pass through the ResNet backbone in a single efficient forward call. The `view(N, T, -1)` call afterwards separates them back, and `feats.mean(dim=1)` pools across time. This is the standard 2D+pool implementation pattern — simple, parallelisable, and compatible with ImageNet-pretrained weights without modification.

questions.question: A (2+1)D factorised convolution splits a 3×3×3 3D conv into which two operations, and what advantage does the factorisation provide?
options: One temporal conv followed by one depthwise conv; the advantage is reduced memory bandwidth
A grouped conv and a 1×1 pointwise conv; the advantage is channel mixing without spatial computation
Two 3D convs with different strides; the advantage is a larger effective receptive field

A spatial 1×3×3 conv followed by a temporal 3×1×1 conv with a BN+ReLU non-linearity in between; the extra non-linearity increases expressive power per parameter and consistently improves accuracy over monolithic 3D convs on benchmarks like Kinetics
questions.explanation: (2+1)D factorises the 3×3×3 kernel into (1×3×3) then (3×1×1). The BN+ReLU between them adds a non-linearity that full 3D convs do not have. On Kinetics, R(2+1)D-34 outperforms an equivalent R3D-34 with fewer parameters — the extra non-linearity does real work, not just parameter reduction.

questions.question: In a video transformer, what does 'divided attention' mean, and what computational problem does it solve compared to joint spatio-temporal attention?
options: Attention applied to half the tokens per layer, reducing VRAM by 2× with no accuracy loss

Each transformer block has two attention modules: one over tokens at the same spatial position across time (temporal attention), then one over tokens at the same timestep across space (spatial attention) — breaking O((T·H·W)²) joint attention into O(T²) + O((H·W)²) per block
Attention applied only during training and replaced with a lookup table at inference
Attention that skips alternate transformer layers to reduce depth while preserving width
questions.explanation: Full joint spatio-temporal attention is O((T·H·W)²), which is infeasible for long videos. Divided attention (TimeSformer) alternates temporal and spatial attention within each block, reducing cost to O(T² + (H·W)²). It trades a theoretical expressivity loss for tractable training; empirically divided attention matches or beats joint attention on most benchmarks.
